title: Cordia speciosa
taxonomy: Cordia speciosa is classified under the family Boraginaceae, which is a group of flowering plants commonly known as borage or forget-me-not family. It belongs to the genus Cordia, which comprises about 100 species of flowering plants. The genus Cordia is part of the order Lamiales, a large and diverse group of flowering plants. Cordia speciosa is native to tropical regions of the Americas and the Caribbean. Its classification is also sometimes placed within the family Cordiaceae.

intro: Cordia speciosa, also known as the "Queens Cordia" or "Flame of the Forest," is a deciduous tree native to the tropical regions of Central and South America. The plant has been traditionally used in folk medicine to treat various health issues, including fever, cough, and digestive problems, due to its purported anti-inflammatory, antimicrobial, and antiseptic properties. The bioactive constituents of Cordia speciosa include flavonoids, glycosides, and terpenoids, which contribute to its medicinal properties. In traditional herbal medicine, Cordia speciosa is often prepared as an infusion, decoction, or tincture to treat various ailments, and its bark, leaves, and flowers are commonly used in the production of teas, syrups, and capsules.
